=== 1900 – Fewer talks, more distance === January 1900 began with a longer version, and more in her own words, of the ghost story in Ford's home - and carried in the ''New York Tribune''.<ref>{{cite news |title=Mrs. Ford's Ghost Story |newspaper=New-York Tribune |location=New York, NY|page=29 |date=January 7, 1900 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2171540/later_bahai_mary_hanford_ford_article/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> A little more than a week later Ford gave a talk on [[Bret Harte]] for the Arché club.<ref>{{cite news |title=The Arche club will meet ...|newspaper=The Inter Ocean |location=Chicago, IL|page=7 |date=January 19, 1900 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2171541/later_bahai_mary_hanford_ford_gives/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> In a couple days she shared a stage with another speaker on the [[Fabian Society]],<ref>{{cite news |title=The Independent Penwoman's club will ...|newspaper=The Inter Ocean |location=Chicago, IL|page=18 |date=January 21, 1900 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2171543/later_bahai_mary_hanford_ford_coruns/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> and another on [[Bret Harte]].<ref>{{cite news |title=The Arché club will meet ...|newspaper=Chicago Daily Tribune |location=Chicago, IL|pages=38 |date=January 21, 1900 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2359099/later_bahai_mary_h_ford_gives_talk_on/ |access-date=May 5, 2015}}</ref>
{{Location map+|United States|width=300|float=right|caption=Travels of Mary Hanford Ford in 1900|places= {{Location map~|United States|label=[[Freeport, Illinois|Freeport]]|position=right|lat=40.0201736|long=-88.0414}} {{location map~|United States|label=[[Topeka, Kansas|Topeka]]|position=left|lat=39.0023 |long=-95.0287}} {{location map~|United States|label=[[Marshall, Michigan|Marshall]]|position=top|lat=42.01142 |long=-84.04014}} {{location map~|United States|label=[[Ottumwa, Iowa|Ottumwa]]|position=top|lat=41.00054 |long=-92.01728}} {{location map~|United States|label=[[Fort Wayne, Indiana|Fort Wayne]]|position=right|lat=41.00335 |long=-85.0058}} {{location map~|United States|label=[[Sedalia, Missouri|Sedalia]]|position=right|lat=38.02929 |long=-93.00963}} {{location map~|United States|label=[[Lynn, Massachusetts|Lynn]]|position=right|lat=42.01944 |long=-70.0396}} }}
In February a poem of hers was published in the ''New York Times'' echoed from the ''Chicago Post'' - "The song unsung".<ref>{{cite news |title=The song unsung |newspaper=New York Times |location=New York, NY|page=22 |date=February 11, 1900 |url=https://newspaperarchive.com/us/new-york/new-york/new-york-times/1900/02-11/page-22 |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> A week later she gives a talk on [[William Dean Howells]] for the Arché club.<ref>{{cite news |title=There will be a lecture on William Dean Howells ...|newspaper=The Inter Ocean |location=Chicago, IL|page=30 |date=February 11, 1900 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2171545/later_bahai_mary_hanford_ford_talk_on/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=The Arché club ...|newspaper=Chicago Daily Tribune |location=Chicago, IL|page=8 |date=February 12, 1900 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2172711/later_bahai_mary_hanford_ford_gives/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> She closes out February with a talk on [[James Abbott McNeill Whistler|Whistler]].<ref>{{cite news |title=The Chicago Culture club will ...|newspaper=The Inter Ocean |location=Chicago, IL|page=18 |date=February 25, 1900 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2171546/later_bahai_mary_hanford_ford_talk_on/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> Nearly two weeks later she was in Fort Wayne,<ref>* {{cite news |title=Thursday- The Society of Art will ...|newspaper=The Fort Wayne Sentinel |location=Fort Wayne, IN|page=5 |date=March 12, 1900 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2171547/later_bahai_mary_hanford_ford_talk_on/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=Mr. and Mrs. W. P. Cooper last evening ...|newspaper=The Fort Wayne Sentinel |location=Fort Wayne, IN|pages=3 |date=March 16, 1900 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2452060/soon_bahai_mary_hanford_ford_gives/ |access-date=May 20, 2015}}</ref> before heading back to New York.<ref>{{cite news |title=Mrs. Mary Ford, of Chicago, ...|newspaper=The Fort Wayne News |location=Fort Wayne, IN|page=4 |date=March 16, 1900 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2452030/soon_bahai_mary_hanford_ford_visiting/ |access-date=May 20, 2015}}</ref> There was a district club meeting she spoke at on color,<ref>{{cite news |title=Illinois club women in the Ninth district ...|newspaper=The Courier-Journal |location=Louisville, KY|page=29 |date=April 22, 1900 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2360296/later_bahai_mary_hanford_ford_gives/ |access-date=May 20, 2015}}</ref> and a few days later she was in Dixon, Illinois for a talk each offered on color,<ref>{{cite news |title=End of the Second Annual meeting at Dixon, Ill. |newspaper=Freeport Journal-Standard |location=Freeport, IL|page=8 |date=April 27, 1900 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2172731/later_bahai_mary_hanford_ford_gives/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> and French artists.<ref>{{cite news |title=Phibian art lecture |location=Dixon, IL|page=5 |date=April 28, 1900 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2452082/soon_bahai_mary_hanford_ford_talk_on/ |access-date=May 20, 2015}}</ref> By the end of April she was back in Chicago giving a talk on "American Poets".<ref>{{cite news |title=Mrs. Mary Ford will speak ...|newspaper=The Inter Ocean |location=Chicago, IL|page=33 |date=April 29, 1900 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2360301/later_bahai_mary_hanford_ford_gives/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref>
The next talk, still on color, was in late May, in [[Freeport, Illinois]],<ref>{{cite news |title=Lecture on Color |newspaper=Freeport Daily Journal |location=Freeport, IL|page=4 |date=May 23, 1900 |url=https://newspaperarchive.com/us/illinois/freeport/freeport-daily-journal/1900/05-23/page-4 |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> and she returned a week later to repeat,<ref>* {{cite news |title=Lecture on color |newspaper=Freeport Daily Journal |location=Freeport, IL|page=4 |date=June 1, 1900 |url=https://newspaperarchive.com/us/illinois/freeport/freeport-daily-journal/1900/06-01/page-4 |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=Lecture on color |newspaper=Freeport Daily Journal |location=Freeport, IL|page=1 |date=June 4, 1900 |url=https://newspaperarchive.com/us/illinois/freeport/freeport-daily-journal/1900/06-04/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> and was the center of a reception June 5<ref>{{cite news |title=Reception for Mrs. Ford |newspaper=Freeport Daily Journal - June 4, 1900 - Freeport, United States Of America |location=Freeport, IL|page=4 |date=June 4, 1900 |url=https://newspaperarchive.com/us/illinois/freeport/freeport-daily-journal/1900/06-04/page-4 |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=Lecture on color |newspaper=Freeport Daily Journal |location=Freeport, IL|page=8 |date=June 6, 1900 |url=https://newspaperarchive.com/us/illinois/freeport/freeport-daily-journal/1900/06-06/page-8 |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> directly before going to [[Ottumwa, Iowa]] before the second week of June where she accomplished two series of lectures.<ref>{{cite journal |author=Harriet C. Towner |title=IA|journal=Official Proceedings of the Fifth Biennial of the General Federation of Women's Clubs (June 4–9, 1900) |pages=85–87 |publisher=John Bornman & Son |date=June 1900 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Yk42AQAAMAAJ&pg=RA2-PA86 |access-date=May 4, 2015}}</ref> That summer US Census, Ford and three Missouri-born children lived on 7th St, Hyde Park, IL.<ref name=USCensus1900>{{cite web | url = https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:MS7S-PJF | title = Mary H Ford Census • United States Census | date = June 2, 1900 | via = FamilySearch.org | publisher = US Federal Government | access-date =April 6, 2023}}{{registration required}}</ref> Ford was listed as widowed, having born and had 3 children, not marking lost any in infancy, was noted as a lecturer, son Roland as an artist, while the other two were students at school.
The earliest newspaper coverage of the Baháʼí Faith, a religion she was about to adopt, in the Chicago area - the first group of Baháʼís in the country - occurred from mid-October 1900.<ref>{{cite news |title=Kenosha misses Abdel Karin |newspaper=The Inter Ocean |location=Chicago, IL|page=33 |date=October 14, 1900 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/312399/controversial_aspect_of_early_bahai/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=Babist leader to lecture |newspaper=The Inter Ocean |location=Chicago, IL|page=12 |date=October 19, 1900 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/24880/babibahai_history/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=Teach new religion - two apostles of doctrine of Manifestation in City |newspaper=The Inter Ocean |location=Chicago, IL|page=67 |date=December 16, 1900 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/327915/the_inter_ocean/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> However Ford was out of town - in mid-October Ford began a series of talks in [[Oak Park, Illinois]],<ref>* {{cite news |title=Mrs. Mary H. Ford, popular club lecturer ...|newspaper=Oak Park Vindicator |location=Oak Park, IL|page=1 |date=October 12, 1900 |url=https://newspaperarchive.com/us/illinois/oak-park/oak-park-vindicator/1900/10-12/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=The first of a series of monthly evening meeting of the Soul Culture Club ...|newspaper=Oak Park Vindicator |location=Oak Park, IL|page=5 |date=October 19, 1900 |url=https://newspaperarchive.com/us/illinois/oak-park/oak-park-vindicator/1900/10-19/page-5 |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=The first of a series of monthly evening meeting of the Soul Culture Club ...|newspaper=Oak Park Vindicator |location=Oak Park, IL|page=5 |date=October 26, 1900 |url=https://newspaperarchive.com/us/illinois/oak-park/oak-park-vindicator/1900/10-26/page-5 |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> mid-November successively in Kansas City,<ref>{{cite news |title=Mrs. Mary H. Ford's Reading |newspaper=Kansas City Star |location=Kansas City, MO|pages=1 |date=November 13, 1900 |url= <!--|access-date=May 21, 2015-->}}</ref> [[Bloomington, Illinois]],<ref>{{cite news |title=The first of a series of three lectures ...|newspaper=Bloomington Weekly Pantagraph |location=Bloomington, IL|pages=15 |date=November 16, 1900 |url=https://newspaperarchive.com/us/illinois/bloomington/bloomington-weekly-pantagraph/1900/11-16/page-15 |access-date=May 21, 2015}}</ref> and then [[Sedalia, Missouri]].<ref>{{cite news |title=Mrs. Mary Hanford Ford of Chicago ...|newspaper=Sedalia Evening Sentinel |location=Sedalia, MO|page=5 |date=November 18, 1900 |url=https://newspaperarchive.com/us/missouri/sedalia/sedalia-evening-sentinel/1900/11-18/page-5 |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref>
She wrote the introduction to ''The story of Abraham Lincoln; or, The journey from the log cabin to the White House'' by Eleanor Gridley.<ref>{{cite book |author=Eleanor Gridley |title=The story of Abraham Lincoln; or, The journey from the log cabin to the White House |chapter=Introduction |date=1907 |orig-year=1900 |pages=3–4 |publisher=M.A. Donohue |chapter-url=http://babel.hathitrust.org/cgi/pt?id=inu.32000001366725;view=1up;seq=15}}</ref>
==Finding the Baháʼís==
=== 1901 – A year of changes ===
Like the recent Januaries, Ford's ghost-story home was referred to by another newspaper story; this time someone was seeking to live in one like hers. It turned out that Ford's family was no longer living there.<ref>* {{cite news |title=Seeks a haunted house |newspaper=The Inter Ocean |location=Chicago, IL|page=7 |date=January 22, 1901 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2172736/later_bahai_mary_hanford_ford/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=Finds a 'Haunted House' - Police captain O'Neill responds to Mrs. Stewart |newspaper=Chicago Daily Tribune |location=Chicago, IL|page=5 |date=January 23, 1901 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2172763/later_bahai_mary_hanford_ford_ghost/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=Ghosts dwell in Chicago |newspaper=Chicago Daily Tribune |location=Chicago, IL|page=45 |date=January 27, 1901 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2172862/later_bahai_mary_hanford_ford_noted_in/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=Where the ghost walks |newspaper=Chicago Daily Tribune |location=Chicago, IL|page=45 |date=January 27, 1901 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2172871/ttcture_of_home_where_later_bahai_mary/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> Her household was noted as an eclectic mix of family and boarders - her three children, Roland, Lynette and Gareth; a Germon woman and her young child; of an American woman and her son; of a young African or African American student of Shakespeare.<ref name=Whitehead/>
In the Winter of 1900-1901 Ford took a comparative religion class and encountered the [[Baháʼí Faith]].<ref name=Whitehead/> This could have been, for example, through the University of Chicago, through "Vesper Services" lectures,<ref>{{cite book |author=University of Chicago |title=University Record |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ZG_OAAAAMAAJ&pg=PA298 |year=1900 |publisher=University of Chicago Press |page=298}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |title=Annual Register Vol 1 No 8 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pzY6AQAAMAAJ&pg=PA208 |year=1901 |pages=208–209}}</ref> or academic classes (for example "Religions of ancient India and Persia").<ref>{{cite book |author=University of Chicago |title=Annual Register |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=IEU9AQAAMAAJ&pg=PA196 |year=1900 |publisher=1893-1930. |pages=196–198}}</ref> [[John Henry Barrows]] was a staff member and had been deeply involved in the [[Parliament of the World's Religions#1893 Parliament|1893 World Parliament of Religions]], where the Baháʼí Faith was mentioned,<ref>{{cite news |title=Afternoon Session - Henry H. Jessup, D. D., Makes an eloquent and instructive address |newspaper=The Inter Ocean |location=Chicago, IL|page=2 |date=September 24, 1893 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/312321/prominent_perhaps_1st_newspaper/ |access-date=April 20, 2015}}</ref> and that winter Charles Cuthbert Hall had been hired by the university,<ref>{{cite news |title=Dr. Charles Cuthbert Hall ...|newspaper=The Hutchinson News |location=Hutchinson, KS|pages=3 |date=February 28, 1900 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2286801/charles_cuthbert_hall_appointed_haskell/ |access-date=April 25, 2015}}</ref> being appointed to an endowed lectureship based on the enthusiasm of the Parliament of Religions.<ref>{{cite news |title=The late Mrs. Carlina E. Haskell, of Michigan City ...|newspaper=New-York Tribune |location=New York, NY|page=8 |date=May 12, 1900 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2286658/caroline_haskell_endowment_for/ |access-date=April 20, 2015}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=Scholarships, Fellowships, Lectureships, and Prizes; Building for a long future: The University of Chicago and its Donors, 1889-1930 |publisher=The University of Chicago Library |url=https://www.lib.uchicago.edu/e/webexhibits/building/scholarships.html |access-date=May 23, 2015}}</ref> It wouldn't be the only such series to be undertaken.<ref>{{cite news |title=Religions of the world - series of lectures to be given by Dr. Tompkins |newspaper=Belvidere Daily Republican |location=Belvidere, IL|page=2 |date=November 12, 1900 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2286718/comparative_religion_course_based_on/ |access-date=April 20, 2015}}</ref> However, in addition, whether Ford knew it or not, Baháʼís had arrived in Chicago who were sent by [[ʻAbdu'l-Bahá]] and began public presentations on the religion in December 1900 and January 1901.<ref>*{{cite news |title=Teach new religion |newspaper=The Inter Ocean |location=Chicago, IL|page=67 |date=December 16, 1900 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/695115/1900_dec_16_teach_new_religion_from/ |access-date=April 20, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=Teachers from Akka ...|newspaper=Chicago Daily Tribune |location=Chicago, IL|page=32 |date=January 27, 1901 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2366276/teachers_from_akka_manifestation/ |access-date=April 20, 2015}}</ref>
Ford's ex-husband, Smith Moses Ford, died April 10.<ref>* {{cite news |title=Smith Moses Ford ...|newspaper=The Kansas City Gazette |location=Kansas City, KS|page=1 |date=April 11, 1901 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2286182/smith_moses_ford_ex_of_mary_hanford/ |access-date=April 20, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=Smith Moses Ford, a well known ...|newspaper=Atchison Daily Globe |location=Atchison, KS|page=4 |date=April 12, 1901 |url=https://newspaperarchive.com/us/kansas/atchison/atchison-daily-globe/1901/04-12/page-4 |access-date=April 20, 2015}}</ref>
The ''New York Times'' noted her selling an apartment in New York to her son in July.<ref>{{cite news |title=132 St 273 West ...|newspaper=The New York Times |location=New York, NY|page=10 |date=July 4, 1901 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2172905/later_bahai_mary_hanford_ford_selling/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref>
After the comparative religion class in Chicago, Ford heard that more about the religion was to be learned at [[Green Acre Baháʼí School]]<ref name=Whitehead/> at the northern fringe of [[Greater Boston]]. [[Sarah Farmer]], founder of the school, was publicly linked with the religion in June after she had found truth in various religions and quasi-religious groups.<ref name=LillianGray>* {{cite news |author=Lillian Gray |title=Sarah Farmer and the Greenacre cult |newspaper=The Wilkes-Barre Record |location=Wilkes-Barre, PA|page=12 |date=June 22, 1901 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2286337/bahai_sarah_farmer_and_greenacre_story/ |access-date=April 25, 2015}} * {{cite news |author=Lillian Gray |title=Sarah Farmer and the Green Acre Cult |newspaper=Evening Report |location=Lebanon, PA|page=6 |date=June 22, 1901 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2286342/bahai_sarah_farmer_and_greenacre_story/ |access-date=April 25, 2015}} * {{cite news |author=Lillian Gray |title=Sarah Farmer and the Greenacre Cult |newspaper=The Fort Wayne Journal-Gazette |location=Fort Wayne, IN|page=11 |date=June 23, 1901 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2286351/bahai_sarah_farmer_and_greenacre_story/ |access-date=April 25, 2015}} * {{cite news |author=Lillian Gray |title=Sarah Farmer and the Greenacre cult |newspaper=San Antonio Express |location=San Antonio, TX|page=14 |date=June 23, 1901 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2366282/bahai_sarah_farmer_and_greenacre_story/ |access-date=April 25, 2015}} * {{cite news |author=Lillian Gray |title=Sarah Farmer and the Greenacre cult |newspaper=The Pittsburgh Press |location=Pittsburgh, PA|page=16 |date=June 23, 1901 |url=https://news.google.com/newspapers?nid=1144&dat=19010623&id=PhIbAAAAIBAJ&pg=3238,5066743&hl=en |access-date=April 25, 2015}}</ref> But of the Baháʼí Faith, it was explained, "... she has found the common faith in which all devout souls may unite and yet be free."<ref name=LillianGray/> It was then announced Green Acre would be a place to learn of the religion, run in parallel with the other classes already established, but for free.<ref>{{cite journal |last=Richardson |first=Robert P. |title=The Rise and Fall of the Parliament of Religions at Greenacre |journal=The Open Court |volume=45 |issue=3 |pages=129–166 |date=March 1931 |url=http://bahai-library.com/richardson_parliament_religions_greenacre |access-date=November 4, 2014}}</ref> [[James Thompson Bixby|James T. Bixby]], who had written previous on the religion's history,<ref>{{cite journal |author=James T. Bixby |author-link=James Thompson Bixby |title=Babism and the Bab |journal=The New World; A Quarterly Review of Religion, Ethics, and Theology |volume=6 |issue=24 |pages=722–750 |date=December 1897 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=s6svAAAAYAAJ&q=editions%3Asv5VcvGUqgcC&pg=PA722 |access-date=May 22, 2015}}</ref> (which Ford herself would soon write differently),<ref name=Rose>{{cite book |author=Mary Hanford Finney Ford |title=The Oriental Rose: Or, The Teachings of Abdul Baha which Trace the Chart of "the Shining Pathway" |url=http://bahai-library.com/ford_oriental_rose |year=1910 |publisher=Broadway Publishing Company}}</ref> was presenting on the religion,<ref>{{cite news |title=Conference School of Comparative Religion |newspaper=Cambridge Tribune |location=Cambridge MA|page=5 |date=May 11, 1901 |url=http://cambridge.dlconsulting.com/cgi-bin/cambridge?a=d&d=Tribune19010511-01.2.45# |access-date=June 1, 2015}}</ref> but [[Mírzá Abu'l-Faḍl]], among the most scholarly trained Baháʼís of the time, was there.<ref name=LillianGray/><ref name=Stockman>{{cite book |author=Robert H. Stockman |title=The Baháʼí Faith in America: Early expansion, 1900-1912 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=WMl-PgAACAAJ |year=1985 |publisher=Baháʼí Pub. Trust |isbn=978-0-85398-388-0 |pages=80, 145–147, 190}}</ref><ref name=Peculiar>* {{cite news |title=Peculiar faith of the Babist sect as revealed by a woman |newspaper=The Inter Ocean |location=Chicago, IL|page=48 |date=December 1, 1901 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/24940/babibahai_history_reports_and/ |access-date=April 25, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=Visit to the Babist Chief |newspaper=The Sun |location=New York, NY|page=6 |date=November 24, 1901 |url=http://fultonhistory.com/Newspaper%209/New%20York%20NY%20Sun/New%20York%20NY%20Sun%201901%20Grayscale/New%20York%20NY%20Sun%201901%20Jan-Dec%20Grayscale%20-%204309.pdf |access-date=April 25, 2015}}</ref> Ali Kuli Khan, to serve as his translator, arrived in the United States in June.<ref>{{cite news |title=Came on the Minnehaha |newspaper=The Brooklyn Daily Eagle |location=Brooklyn, NY|page=24 |date=June 24, 1901 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2155418/arrival_of_bahai_ali_kuli_khan/ |access-date=April 25, 2015}}</ref> A Baháʼí publication notes Abu'l-Faḍl's talk titled "Utterances of Baháʼu'lláh" and Ford herself giving a talk "Lectures in Literature".<ref name=centenary>{{cite book |title=The Baháʼí centenary, 1844-1944: a record of America's response to Baháʼo'lláh's call to the realization of the oneness of mankind, to commemorate the one hundredth anniversary of the birth of the Baháʼí faith |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=s1fYAAAAMAAJ |year=1944 |publisher=Baháʼí publishing committee |pages=212–214}}</ref> Green Acre closed for the season in September.<ref>* {{cite news |title=Greenacre Inn will closes |newspaper=The Portsmouth Herald |location=Portsmouth, NH|page=6 |date=September 10, 1901 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2366234/greenacre_inn_will_close_in_a_week/ |access-date=April 25, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=Miss Sarah J Farmer ...|newspaper=The Portsmouth Herald |location=Portsmouth, NH|page=6 |date=September 28, 1901 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/658656/1901_sept_28_sarah_farmer_will_spend/ |access-date=April 25, 2015}}</ref><!-- {{Location map+|United States Greater Boston|width=400|float=right|caption=known locations of talks of Ford in 1895|places= {{Location map~|United States Greater Boston|label=|position=top|lat=43.11146|long=-70.79446}} }} -->[[File:Greaterboston2.png|thumbnail|Green Acre is on the southern border of Maine and New Hampshire]] Abu'l-Faḍl had accompanied Anton Hadded, the first Baháʼí to live in the United States, on his return trip to America.<ref name=Stockman/> They too had been sent by then head of the religion, ʻAbdu'l-Bahá.<ref name=Peculiar/> The later well known Baháʼí [[Agnes Baldwin Alexander]] was there.<ref>{{cite book |last=Alexander |first=Agnes Baldwin |editor=Barbara Sims |title=History of the Baháʼí Faith in Japan 1914-1938 |publisher=Japan Baháʼí Publishing Trust |year=1977 |location=Osaka, Japan |page=21 |url=http://bahai-library.com/alexander_history_bahai_japan}}</ref> It was at these classes with Abu'l-Faḍl that Ford is considered to have joined the religion,<ref name=Whitehead/><ref name=Stockman/> at the age of 44.
In ''Arches of the Years'' Alice Breed's granddaughter [[Marzieh Gail]] recalled: "When (Ali Kuli Khan) was translating for Abu'l-Faḍl at Green Acre, Mary Hanford Ford introduced him to Alice Breed".<ref name=Arches>{{cite book |author=Marzieh Gail |title=Arches of the Years |url=http://bahairesearch.com/english/Baha'i/Baha'i_Studies/Marzieh_Gail/Arches%20of%20the%20Years.aspx |year=1991 |publisher=Ronald |isbn=978-0-85398-325-5 |pages=see chapters 7, 9, 27, 45}}</ref> Gail also noted Ford was the "spiritual mother" of Alice Breed - that Ford lead her to the religion.<ref>{{cite book |author1=Marzieh Gail |author2=Ali-Kuli Khan |title=Summon up remembrance |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=NBINAAAAIAAJ |date=December 31, 1987 |publisher=G. Ronald |isbn=978-0-85398-259-3 |pages=191, 193, 281}}</ref> Ford was visible in the Boston area in October for one talk on color,<ref>{{cite news |title=Discoursed on "Color"; Boston Business league listens to interesting address by Mrs. Ford of Chicago |newspaper=Boston Herald |location =Boston, MA|page=9 |date =October 11, 1901 }}</ref> then a series of talks,<ref>{{cite news |title=A series of 10 talks ...|newspaper=Boston Daily Globe |location=Boston, MA|page=38 |date=October 27, 1901 |url=https://newspaperarchive.com/us/massachusetts/boston/boston-daily-globe/1901/10-27/page-38 |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> and in early November where she gave a talk at [[Chickering Hall, Boston (1901)|Chickering Hall]], Boston, with subjects like "Christ's message and its relation to his time"<ref>{{cite news |title=Mrs. Mary Hanford Ford's course ...|newspaper=Boston Herald |location =Boston, MA|page=36 |date =November 3, 1901 }}</ref> "The Primitive Church, or the Ideal of Brotherhood Love",<ref>{{cite news |title=During six months, ...|newspaper=Boston Post |location=Boston, MA|page=15 |date=November 10, 1901 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2163721/soon_bahai_mary_hanford_ford_gives_talk/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> and "The significance of the Holy Grail" in early December,<ref>{{cite news |title=Mrs. Mary Hanford Ford at her lecture ...|newspaper= Boston Herald |location =Boston, MA|page=34 |date =December 8, 1901 }}</ref> and talks planned into January the next year.<ref>{{cite news |title =Tomorrow forenoon in Chickering Hall ...|newspaper =Boston Herald |location =Boston, MA|page=34 |date =December 15, 1901 }}</ref> In addition Ford also participated in an animal rescue league meeting speaking on "The value of humane education for the young" in mid-November.<ref>{{cite news |title=Homeless pets of Boston; Animal rescue league cared for over 5000 last summer |newspaper=Boston Herald |location =Boston, MA|page=7 |date =November 13, 1901 }}</ref>
In November there was also more coverage of Baháʼís in New York highlighting [[Lua Getsinger]].<ref name=Peculiar/>
===The Boston community=== {{See also|Bahá'í Faith in Greater Boston}} In January news of Farmer's involvement in the religion continued to spread<ref>{{cite journal |title=What is Babism? |journal=The Literary Digest |volume=25 |issue=2 |pages=53–54 |date=January 11, 1902 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=-O05AQAAMAAJ&pg=PA53 |access-date=April 25, 2015}}</ref> as well as of the religion in general,<ref>{{cite journal |author=Kenneth R. Forbes |title=What is Babism? |journal=Mind |volume=9 |issue=4 |pages=271–276 |date=January 1902 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=0CcaAQAAIAAJ&pg=PA271 |access-date=April 25, 2015}}</ref> and Ford was known to have moved to Boston - Ford and the Breed family invited Ali Kuli Khan to move to Boston and together form the [[Baháʼí Faith in Greater Boston|first active community of Baháʼís there]].<ref name=Stockman/>
News that Ford was working on translating Charles Paul de Kock's began to be noted in 1902,<ref>* {{cite news |title=The novels, tales, and vaudevilles of Charles ...|newspaper=New York Times |location=New York, NY|page=28 |date=February 1, 1902 |url=https://newspaperarchive.com/us/new-york/new-york/new-york-times/1902/02-01/page-28 |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=Of all the surprising literary revivals ...|newspaper=The Times |location=Philadelphia, PA|page=16 |date=March 22, 1902 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2163730/soon_bahai_mary_hanford_ford_publishes/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=Apotheosis of P. de Kock. |newspaper=New York Times |location=New York, NY|page=25 |date=June 5, 1902 |url=https://newspaperarchive.com/us/new-york/new-york/new-york-times/1902/07-05/page-25 |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title =Books That Only Wealthy Can Buy. Ten Persons Invest $50,000 Each in an Edition of 100 Volumes |newspaper =Duluth News-Tribune |location =Duluth, MN|page=9 (Magazine Section) |date =December 21, 1902 }}</ref> and 1903.<ref>{{cite news |title=The Writings of Paul de Kock |newspaper=The New York Herald |location=New York, NY|page=15 |date=February 8, 1903 |url=http://fultonhistory.com/Newspaper%2014/New%20York%20NY%20Herald/New%20York%20NY%20Herald%201903/New%20York%20NY%20Herald%201903%20-%201305.pdf |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> News spread (in Kansas City and Kansas) that the Baháʼí Faith was being promoted in New England,<ref>* {{cite news |title=Preach a new religion - Persian Missionaries seek converts in New England |newspaper=The Rising Son |location=Kansas City, MO|page=3 |date=September 25, 1903 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2286302/bahais_noted_in_new_england_mirza/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=Preach a new religion - Persian Missionaries seek converts in New England |newspaper=The Olsburg Gazette |location=Olsburg, KS|page=3 |date=October 2, 1903 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2286307/bahais_noted_in_new_england_mirza/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> and in July Ford was giving a series of talks at Greenacre.<ref>{{cite news |title= Mrs. Mary Hanford Ford of the Chicago Fortnightly Club ...|newspaper=Boston Herald |location =Boston, MA|page=33 |date =July 26, 1903 }}</ref> In September a talk of hers, amplified by quotes translated by Ali Kuli Khan from Mirza Abu Fazl, was profiled in the ''Boston Journal'' - it reviewed spirituality and history as a call for a new religion.<ref>{{cite news |title=Eastern Occultists See New Religion Twentieth Century Ideas and Their Relation to the Old Discussed |newspaper=Boston Journal |location =Boston, MA|page= (Second Section,) 7 |date =September 13, 1903 }}</ref> She was then published giving talks in Boston in October,<ref>{{cite news |title=Mrs. Mary Hanford Ford opens her series ...|newspaper=Boston Herald |location =Boston, MA|page=33 |date = October 18, 1903 }}</ref> and November.<ref>{{cite news |title=Mrs Mary Hanford Ford of Boston will give ...|newspaper=Boston Globe |location=Boston, MA|page=85 |date=November 1, 1903 |url=https://newspaperarchive.com/us/massachusetts/boston/boston-globe/1903/11-01/page-85 |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> By December Baháʼís in Chicago knew she was a Baháʼí and working with [[Sarah Farmer]] on projects.<ref>{{cite web |last=Stockman |first=Robert |title=Notes, 1903-1904 |work=American Baha'i History, 1892-1912 - Robert Stockman's Notes in the National Baháʼí Archives |date=1986 |url=https://www.h-net.org/~bahai/notes/vol2/chs.1903-04.html |access-date=April 25, 2015}}</ref> A talk of hers in December was also noted in the ''Boston Herald''.<ref>{{cite news |title=Mrs. Mary Hanford Ford gave her closing lecture ...|newspaper=Boston Herald |location =Boston, MA|page=32 |date =December 6, 1903 }}</ref>
In March 1904 someone gave a talk on Ford's books in Kansas,<ref>{{cite news |title=Mrs. L. W. Higgins gave a very fine program ...|newspaper=The Kinsley Graphic |location=Kinsley, KS|page=1 |date=March 11, 1904 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2172980/reading_from_bahai_mary_hanford_fords/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> while news of another book of Ford's came out - ''Legends of [[Parsifal]]''<ref>{{cite book |author=Mary Hanford Finney Ford |title=The Legends of Parsifal |url=https://archive.org/details/legendsparsifal01fordgoog |year=1904 |publisher=H.M. Caldwell Company}}</ref> - and news of it continues progressively over the year.<ref>{{cite news |title="Parsifal" Legends - some forthcoming novels - new editions |newspaper=New York Times |location=New York, NY|page=21 |date=March 26, 1904 |url=https://newspaperarchive.com/us/new-york/new-york/new-york-times/1904/03-26/page-21 |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=The Legends of Parsifal |newspaper=New York Times |location=New York, NY|page=35 |date=April 2, 1904 |url=https://newspaperarchive.com/us/new-york/new-york/new-york-times/1904/04-02/page-35 |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=Strenuous Epigrams of Theodore Roosevelt. The Legends of Parsifal ...|newspaper=Brooklyn Life |location=Brooklyn, NY|page=8 |date=June 25, 1904 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2163735/bahai_mary_hanford_ford_publishes_book/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=The Legend of Parsifal |newspaper=The Syracuse Herald |location=Syracuse, NY|page=4 |date=July 10, 1904 |url=http://fultonhistory.com/Newspaper%2016/Syracuse%20NY%20Herald/Syracuse%20NY%20Herald%201904/Syracuse%20NY%20Herald%201904%20a%20-%201943.pdf |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=Books and Magazines; "The Legends of Parsifal"... |newspaper=The New York Dramatic Mirror |location=New York, NY|page=7 |date=August 6, 1904 |url=http://fultonhistory.com/Newspaper%2010/New%20York%20NY%20Dramatic%20Mirror/New%20York%20NY%20Dramatic%20Mirror%201904%20Jun%20Apr%201905%20Grayscale/New%20York%20NY%20Dramatic%20Mirror%201904%20Jun%20Apr%201905%20Grayscale%20-%200220.pdf |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=To the much that has been written ...|newspaper=The San Francisco Call |location=San Francisco, California |page=• First Edition |date=August 14, 1904 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2163751/bahai_mary_hanford_ford_writing/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=(continued) |newspaper=The San Francisco Call |location=San Francisco, California |page=• First Edition |date=August 14, 1904 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2163754/bahai_mary_hanford_ford_writing/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=The Parsifal Legends |newspaper=The New York Press |location=New York, NY|page=7 |date=August 20, 1904 |url=http://fultonhistory.com/Newspaper%2018/New%20York%20NY%20Press/New%20York%20NY%20Press%201904/New%20York%20NY%20Press%201904%20-%203100.pdf |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=Parsifal; Guileless Fool ...|newspaper=The Washington Post |location=Washington, District of Columbia |page=11 |date=September 17, 1904 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2172998/bahai_mary_hanford_fords_book_noted/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=The Legends of Parsifal |newspaper=The Houston Post |location=Houston, TX|page=42 |date=November 27, 1904 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2163758/bahai_mary_hanford_ford_writing/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=Timely publications |newspaper=Kansas City Daily Gazette |location=Kansas City, KS|page=1 |date=December 9, 1904 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2163766/bahai_mary_hanford_ford_writing/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=Two other recent little books ... |newspaper=The Evening Post |location=New York, NY|page=5 |date=December 24, 1904 |url=http://fultonhistory.com/Newspaper%2010/New%20York%20NY%20Evening%20Post/New%20York%20NY%20Evening%20Post%201904%20Grayscale/New%20York%20NY%20Evening%20Post%201904%20Grayscale%20-%204368.pdf |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> Her Kock books also came out.<ref>{{cite book |author=Paul de Kock |title=The works of Charles Paul de Kock |url=https://archive.org/details/workscharlespau23kockgoog |year=1904 |publisher=The Frederick J. Quinby company}}</ref> Still news on her "The Holy Grail" appears.<ref>{{cite news |title=New Literature |newspaper=Boston Daily Globe |location=Boston, MA|page=16 |date=May 6, 1904 |url=https://newspaperarchive.com/us/massachusetts/boston/boston-daily-globe/1904/05-06/page-16 |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref>
In October Khan and Florence Breed were married.<ref>{{cite news |title=Persian weds American Girl - Bostonian is bride of Ali Kuli Khan, oriental mystic |newspaper=The Indianapolis News |location=Indianapolis, IN|page=7 |date=October 31, 1904 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2288566/bahais_ali_kuli_khan_marries_florence/ |access-date=April 25, 2015}}</ref>
In December Ford gave her ''Grail'' talk to the Lethren club in Boston.<ref>{{cite news |title=The Lethren club will ...|newspaper=Boston Daily Globe |location=Boston, MA|page=26 |date=December 8, 1904 |url=https://newspaperarchive.com/us/massachusetts/boston/boston-daily-globe/1904/12-08/page-26 |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=The Lethren club held a meeting ...|newspaper=Boston Daily Globe |location=Boston, MA|date=December 11, 1904 |url=https://newspaperarchive.com/us/massachusetts/boston/boston-daily-globe/1904/12-11/page-74 |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref>
In the Spring, Ford gave a talk in Springfield on art.<ref>{{cite news |title=The Amherst woman's club ...|newspaper=Springfield Republican |location =Springfield, MA|page=8 |date =March 20, 1905 }}</ref> Ali Kuli Khan and perhaps others passed most of the summer of 1905 in Green Acre.<ref name=Leave>{{cite news |title=To leave for Persia |newspaper=The Portsmouth Herald |location=Portsmouth, NH|page=4 |date=April 2, 1906 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/296930/bahai_ali_kuli_khan_and_family_noted/ |access-date=April 25, 2015}}</ref> Ford was listed as an honorary member of the Arché Club that year.<ref>{{cite book |title=The Arché Club |publisher=Arché Club |series=Yearbook |volume=1904-1905 |date=1905 |location=Chicago, IL|page=34 |url=http://babel.hathitrust.org/cgi/pt?id=wu.89072935364;view=1up;seq=40 |last1=Arché Club (Chicago |first1=Ill) }}</ref>
In April 1906 Ali Kuli Khan gave a talk at the Breed home and then he and his new wife left for Persia.<ref name=Leave/> During their trip they first went on pilgrimage to meet ʻAbdu'l-Bahá where Mrs. Ali Kuli Khan later recalled these words of ʻAbdu'l-Bahá - "It is true that Mrs. Ford has served humanity long and faithfully. Now tell her, if she will arise to serve the Cause of Baháʼu'lláh with equal zeal and fidelity, her name will be mentioned in all the worlds of God."<ref name=BWvo7Bio/>
The August issue of ''Brush and Pencil'' had an article "American Art Eminently Distinctive" by Ford.<ref>{{cite journal |author=Mary H. Ford |title=American Art Eminently Distinctive |journal=Brush and Pencil |volume=18 |issue=2 |pages=61–63 |date=August 1906 |jstor=25504032 |doi=10.2307/25504032 <!--|access-date=May 21, 2015-->}}</ref> The December issue of [[Success (magazine)]] had a short story by Ford: "Love enough for all".<ref>* {{cite news |title=Magazine Notes |newspaper=Niagara Falls Gazette |location=Niagara Falls, NY|page=3 |date=December 19, 1906 |url=http://fultonhistory.com/Newspaper%208/Niagara%20Falls%20NY%20Gazette/Niagara%20Falls%20NY%20Gazette%201906%20Jul-Dec%20Grayscale/Niagara%20Falls%20NY%20Gazette%201906%20Jul-Dec%20Grayscale%20-%201138.pdf |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=In the field of Literature; Success |newspaper=The Argus |volume=56 |issue=38 |location=Rock Island, IL|page=4 |date=November 30, 1906 |url=http://idnc.library.illinois.edu/cgi-bin/illinois?a=d&d=RIA19061130.1.4&srpos=3&e=--1870---1940--en-20--1--txt-txIN-%22Mary+Hanford+Ford%22------# |access-date=April 25, 2015}}</ref> The story began with a husband swearing about "modern club-woman" with contrary ideas on raising children, but as the story continues the impatient mother gets in an accident who did not deal well with the child that adored her yet kept her from clubs. While recuperating she marvels at the housewife who can hold a conversation with guests and husband, children entertained, house well kept, and she well loved. The recuperating lady dreams and sees marvels all giving gifts to this one who loves all and is loved.<ref>{{cite journal |last=Hanford Ford |first=Mary |editor1=[[Orison Swett Marden]] |editor2=Robert MacKay |title=Love enough for all |journal=Success Magazine |volume=9 |issue=151 |pages=852–853 |location=New York, NY|date=December 1906 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=t3ZNAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA852 |access-date=April 25, 2015}} * {{cite news |title =Recent publications |newspaper =Times-Picayune |location =New Orleans, LA|page=28 |date =December 2, 1906 }} * {{cite news |title =Contents of the December "Success Magazine"...|newspaper =Morning Journal and Courier |location =New Haven, CT|page=10 |date =December 8, 1906 }}</ref> In the Fall of 1906 Ford had a story published in the [[Overland Monthly]].<ref>{{cite journal |author=Mary Hanford Ford |title=Country Life in North Carolina |editor1=Samuel Carson |journal=Overland Monthly |volume=48 |issue=5 |pages=489–494 |date=November 1906 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=WLpUAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA489 |access-date=April 25, 2015}}</ref>
By the Winter of 1906 [[Louis Bourgeois (architect)|Louis Bourgeois]], later architect of the [[Baháʼí House of Worship|Baháʼí House of Worship in Wilmette, Illinois]], and his wife had joined the religion after having "come into association with the Baha'i Faith through Marie Watson and Mary Hanford Ford."<ref>[https://www.h-net.org/~bahai/notes/bourgeoi.htm Bourgeois, Jean-Baptiste Louis (1856-1930) - Designer of Mashriqu'l-Adhkar at Wilmette, Illinois, United States of America], R. Jackson Armstrong-Ingram, Research Notes in Shaykhi, Babi and Baha'i Studies, Vol. 1, no. 7 (September 1997)</ref> But by late December 1906, Ford and her son had moved back to Kansas City.<ref>{{cite news |title=In Society; Mrs. Mary Hanford Ford ...|newspaper=Kansas City Star |location =Kansas City, MO|page= (Second section,) 2 |date =December 23, 1906 }}</ref>
==Back to Missouri and pilgrimage== A week after her arrival in Kansas City Ford gave two lectures.<ref>* {{cite news |title=In Society; Mrs. Mary Hanford Ford will give a lecture ...|newspaper=Kansas City Star |location =Kansas City, MO|page= (Second section,) 2 |date =December 30, 1906 }} * {{cite news |title=Gossip of Society; Mrs. Mary Hanford Ford will give a lecture ...|newspaper=Kansas City Star |location =Kansas City, MO|page=2 |date = December 31, 1906 }}</ref> Then she spoke on Ibsen at Frazer Hall at the [[University of Kansas]] in January 1907 and she noted she was going abroad.<ref>* {{cite news |title=University Notes |newspaper=Lawrence Daily Journal |location=Lawrence, KS|page=1 |date=January 9, 1907 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2163771/bahai_mary_hanford_ford_profiled/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=Mrs Mary Hanford Ford will speak ...|newspaper=Lawrence Daily World |location=Lawrence, KS|page=3 |date=January 10, 1907 |url=https://newspaperarchive.com/us/kansas/lawrence/lawrence-daily-world/1907/01-10/page-3 |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=Mrs. Mary Hanford Ford lectures ...|newspaper=Lawrence Journal World |location=Lawrence, KS|page=4 |date=January 11, 1907 |url=https://newspaperarchive.com/us/kansas/lawrence/lawrence-journal-world/1907/01-11/page-4 |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> In February Ford addressed comments on each of the artists showing at a university art exhibit,<ref>* {{cite news |title=For Art Exhibit |newspaper=Lawrence Daily World |location=Lawrence, KS|page=5 |date=February 26, 1907 |url=https://newspaperarchive.com/us/kansas/lawrence/lawrence-daily-world/1907/02-26/page-5 |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=For art exhibit |newspaper=Lawrence Daily World |location=Lawrence, KS|page=4 |date=February 26, 1907 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2163777/bahai_mary_hanford_ford_artist/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> and had an article published in ''The Public''.<ref>{{cite journal |author=Mary Hanford Ford |author-link=Mary Hanford Ford |title=House-mother vs Home-mother |journal=The Public |editor1=Louis Freeland Post |editor2=Alice Thatcher Post |volume=9 |issue=461 |pages=1045–1046 |date=February 2, 1907 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nIlGAQAAIAAJ&pg=PA1045 |access-date=May 23, 2015}}</ref> In April Ford was on the program of a Kansas state meeting of women's clubs.<ref>{{cite news |title=Program for the state Meeting |newspaper=The Topeka Daily Capital |location=Topeka, KS|page=15 |date=April 21, 1907 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2163787/bahai_mary_hanford_ford_on_program_of/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=For State meeting - Kansas Federation of women's clubs to be here |newspaper=The Kansas City Kansas Globe |location=Kansas City, KS|page=1 |date=April 27, 1907 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2166736/ford_mh__1907_apr_27__kc__p1/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref>
Ford wrote that she went on [[Baháʼí pilgrimage|pilgrimage]] to meet ʻAbdu'l-Bahá in 1907 traveling over land through Europe, Turkey, and down through Syria,<ref name=SoW24.4>{{cite news |title=An Interview with ʻAbdu'l-Baha |author=Mary Hanford Ford |newspaper=Star of the West |volume=24 |issue=4 |pages=103–107 |date=July 1933 |url=http://starofthewest.info/viewer.erb?vol=24&page=103 |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> at the age of 50. She connected with Baháʼís in Paris on the way to Switzerland/Italy.<ref name=Arches/> Ford later wrote of getting passed "plague and quarantine" and of hearing of fresh Baha'i martyrs while staying in Paris.
The house occupied by ʻAbdu'l-Bahá in Palestine then was the [[Baháʼí World Centre buildings#House of ʻAbdu'lláh Páshá|House of ʻAbdu'lláh Páshá]].
January 1908 began with notice that Ford's talks were the first priority of the federated clubs of Topeka,<ref>{{cite news |title=Executive board of city federation met |newspaper=The Topeka Daily Capital |location=Topeka, KS|page=5 |date=January 8, 1908 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2166717/ford_mh__1908_jan_8__topeka/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> while she was visible in Kansas City,<ref>{{cite news |title=Mrs. J. C. Merine ...|newspaper=The Kansas City Star |location=Kansas City, MO|page=20 |date=January 19, 1908 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/192983/the_kansas_city_star/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> but it was still an open question in early February what would happen.<ref>{{cite news |title=City Federation will meet |newspaper=The Topeka Daily Capital |location=Topeka, KS|page=11 |date=February 2, 1908 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2173009/bahai_mary_hanford_ford_to_give/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> A week later the news is clear - Ford was to come in March for a short series of lectures.<ref>{{cite news |title=Federation is opposed to bill boards |newspaper=The Topeka Daily Capital |location=Topeka, KS|page=5 |date=February 7, 1908 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2166699/ford_mh__1908_feb_7__topeka/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=Mrs Hanford Ford will lecture here |newspaper=The Topeka Daily Capital |location=Topeka, KS|page=11 |date=February 9, 1908 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2166673/ford_mh__1908_feb_9__topeka/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=Art committee will meet |newspaper=The Topeka Daily Capital |location=Topeka, KS|page=8 |date=February 16, 1908 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2166662/ford_mh__1908_feb_16__topeka/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=Mrs Ford of Kansas City will lecture here |newspaper=The Topeka Daily Capital |location=Topeka, KS|page=13 |date=March 1, 1908 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2166645/ford_mh__1908_mar_1__topeka/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> It is said she gave some talks in Kansas City in late February or early March.<ref>{{cite news |title=Mrs Ford's lectures |newspaper=The Topeka Daily Capital |location=Topeka, KS|page=13 |date=March 8, 1908 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2166633/ford_mh__1908_mar_8__topeka/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> The topic of her Topeka talks announced is Chateaux de [[Touraine]]. Final preparations are taken,<ref>{{cite news |title=Board of Directors of city federation will meet |newspaper=The Topeka Daily Capital |location=Topeka, KS|page=5 |date=March 11, 1908 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2166611/ford_mh__1908_mar_11__topeka/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> and the upcoming talks are praised suggesting it has been previewed.<ref>{{cite news |author=Mary Worrall Hudson |title=Mrs. Ford's Lectures |newspaper=The Topeka Daily Capital |location=Topeka, KS|page=4 |date=March 26, 1908 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2163797/bahai_mary_hanford_ford_talks_letter/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> The first talk centers on "[[Château de Chinon|Chinon]] and Loches" and mentions [[Joan of Arc]],<ref>* {{cite news |title=Mrs Ford tells of French architecture |newspaper=The Topeka Daily Capital |location=Topeka, KS|page=6 |date=March 27, 1908 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2166480/ford_mh__1908_mar_27__topeka__i/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=(continued) |newspaper=The Topeka Daily Capital |location=Topeka, KS|page=6 |date=March 27, 1908 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2166489/ford_mh__1908_mar_27__topeka__ii/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> a mention she would later use to example love in religion.<ref name=Crosson2013>{{cite thesis |author=Selena M. Crosson |title=Searching for May Maxwell:Baháʼí Millennial Feminism, Transformative Identity & Globalism in the new World Order Shaping Women's Role in Early Baháʼí Culture 1898-1940 |pages=45, 93, 115–116 |publisher=Department of History University of Saskatchewan Saskatoon, SK, Canada |date=June 2013 |url=http://ecommons.usask.ca/bitstream/handle/10388/ETD-2013-10-1145/CROSSON-DISSERTATION.pdf |access-date=May 23, 2015 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170806021303/https://ecommons.usask.ca/bitstream/handle/10388/ETD-2013-10-1145/CROSSON-DISSERTATION.pdf |archive-date=August 6, 2017 |url-status=dead }}</ref>
Overall the Baháʼís in Chicago heard that Ford was active in Kansas City.<ref>{{cite web |author=Robert Stockman |title=American Baha'i History, 1892-1912 - Robert Stockman's Notes in the National Baháʼí Archives |work=Research Notes |date=1986 |url=https://www.h-net.org/~bahai/notes/vol2/chs.1908.html |access-date=May 23, 2015}}</ref>
In September Ford was living in Kansas City,<ref>{{cite news |title=A friend gave the tip ...|newspaper=The Kansas City Times |location=Kansas City, MO|page=2 |date=September 4, 1908 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2173021/mention_of_bahai_mary_hanford_ford/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> and by November she was noted giving "parlor talks".<ref>{{cite news |title=Parlor talks |newspaper=The Leavenworth Times |location=Leavenworth, KS|page=5 |date=November 26, 1908 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2163808/bahai_mary_hanford_ford_talk_at_home/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=Parlor talks |newspaper=The Leavenworth Times |location=Leavenworth, KS|page=5 |date=November 27, 1908 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2166457/ford_mh__1908_nov_27_leavenworth/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> In December she attended, and her comments are reported for, an exhibition there.<ref>{{cite news |title=George R. Barse's great painting, "The Awakening" |newspaper=The Kansas City Times |location=Kansas City, MO|page=4 |date=December 4, 1908 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2173039/bahai_mary_hanford_ford_comments_on/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref>
In January 1909 Ford was still drawing on her pilgrimage experience referring to descriptions in Turkey and Syria in a talk she gave in [[Leavenworth, Kansas]] – and a series of talks was also announced,<ref>* {{cite news |title=Mrs. Ford's talks |newspaper=The Leavenworth Times |location=Leavenworth, KS|page=5 |date=January 1, 1909 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2173045/bahai_mary_hanford_ford_talk_series/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=Mrs. Mary Ford Lectured |newspaper=The Leavenworth Times |location=Leavenworth, KS|page=8 |date=January 13, 1909 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2173053/bahai_mary_hanford_ford_talk_on_art/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> followed by another focused on Italian cities.<ref>{{cite news |title=Talk |newspaper=The Leavenworth Times |location=Leavenworth, KS|page=5 |date=January 24, 1909 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2173061/bahai_mary_hanford_ford_talks/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=Mrs. Mary H. Ford's ...|newspaper=The Leavenworth Times |location=Leavenworth, KS|page=9 |date=January 31, 1909 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2173064/bahai_mary_hanford_ford_talk_on_art/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=Parlor talk |newspaper=The Leavenworth Times |location=Leavenworth, KS|page=5 |date=February 23, 1909 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2173079/bahai_mary_hanford_ford_talk_series/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> But in February she offers an individual talk as well - "Social adjustment, family relation and revival of neighborship",<ref>{{cite news |title=Talk |newspaper=The Leavenworth Times |location=Leavenworth, KS|page=5 |date=February 9, 1909 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2173074/bahai_mary_hanford_ford_talk_on/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> and offers follow-up talks on the religion.<ref>{{cite news |title=Mrs. Ford's Talks |newspaper=The Leavenworth Post |location=Leavenworth, KS|page=5 |date=February 23, 1909 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2854517/bahai_mary_hanford_ford_talk/ |access-date=July 21, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=Mrs. Ford's talk |newspaper=The Leavenworth Times |location=Leavenworth, KS|page=5 |date= February 27, 1909 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/3692822/bahai_mary_hanford_ford_talk_at_snyder/ |access-date=November 24, 2015}}</ref> After a month another talk was visible,<ref>{{cite news |title=Tuesday |newspaper=The Leavenworth Times |location=Leavenworth, KS|page=9 |date=March 7, 1909 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2173087/bahai_mary_hanford_ford_talk/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> which were then called weekly talks (the next one on American art and craftsman guild,<ref>{{cite news |title=Talk |newspaper=The Leavenworth Times |location=Leavenworth, KS|page=9 |date=March 14, 1909 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2173091/bahai_mary_hanford_ford_talk_series/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> then on called "books as dangerous things").<ref>{{cite news |title=Parlor talk |newspaper=The Leavenworth Times |location=Leavenworth, KS|page=5 |date=March 20, 1909 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2173100/bahai_mary_hanford_ford_talk_series/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref>
Up until mid-June Ford had been a vice president of the Women's Dining Club but on the resignation of the president noting that the club had "... formed among the women who have accomplished things in Kansas City to solve the problems which confront women in this city", Ford was elected president.<ref>{{cite news |title=Women diners elect Mrs. Ford |newspaper=The Kansas City Times |location=Kansas City, MO|page=11 |date=June 22, 1909 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2163811/bahai_mary_hanford_ford_elected/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref>
Later in December Ford spoke for a women's group on strike or worked in a [[sweatshop]] in New York.<ref>{{cite news |title=Votes for women soon will be cry on the East side |newspaper=The New York Press |location=New York, NY|page=5 |date=December 30, 1909 |url=http://fultonhistory.com/Newspaper%2018/New%20York%20NY%20Press/New%20York%20NY%20Press%201909/New%20York%20NY%20Press%201909%20-%204997.pdf |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> She was in fact in suffrage meetings with [[Alva Belmont]].<ref>{{cite news |title=Mrs. Belmont is East Side Guest - Presides at lively meeting of Wage Earners Organization for Equal Rights |newspaper=New York Herald |location=New York, NY|page=5 |date=December 30, 1909 |url=http://fultonhistory.com/Newspaper%2014/New%20York%20NY%20Herald/New%20York%20NY%20Herald%201909/New%20York%20NY%20Herald%201909%20-%207235.pdf |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref>
==A book, abroad, and troubled in Britain in absentia== 1910 starts out noting Ford's daughter Lynette lived in New York by February and Ford was noted visiting over the previous winter finishing work on a book.<ref>* {{cite news |title =Kansas Citians Back East |newspaper =Kansas City Star |location =Kansas City, MO |page =3 |date =February 13, 1910 }} * {{cite news |title=About Mrs. Ford |newspaper=The Topeka Daily Capital |location=Topeka, KS|page=5 |date=February 15, 1910 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2163821/bahai_mary_hanford_ford_profiled_and/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> Ford published ''The Oriental Rose: Or, The Teachings of Abdul Baha which Trace the Chart of "the Shining Pathway"'' in 1910<ref name=Rose/> and it came out before mid-October.<ref>{{cite news |title=The Oriental Rose ...|newspaper=Star of the West |volume=1 |issue=12 |pages=9–10 |date=October 16, 1910 |url=http://starofthewest.info/viewer.erb?vol=01&page=217 |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> In March Ford was elected to the Baháʼí organization "Woman's Board" of New York, a precursor to the [[Spiritual Assembly#Local Spiritual Assemblies|Local Assembly]], and was a delegate to the national convention of Baháʼís.<ref>{{cite news |title=New York City |author=Isabella D. Brittingham |newspaper=Star of the West |volume=1 |issue=1 |pages=17–18 |date=March 21, 1910 |url=http://starofthewest.info/viewer.erb?vol=01&page=17 |access-date=April 28, 2015}}</ref> She visited [[John French Sloan|John Sloan]] in New York in April.<ref>{{cite book |author=Sloan, John |title=New York Scene |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4vWQloPnAacC&pg=PA414 |publisher=Transaction Publishers |isbn=978-1-4128-4929-6 |page=414}}</ref> While in New York, she spoke to an audience about the religion, her pilgrimage,<ref>{{cite news |title=Marvels of Abdul Baha |newspaper=The Illustrated Buffalo Express |location=Buffalo, NY|page=16 |date=April 30, 1910 |url=http://fultonhistory.com/Newpapers%20Disk2/Buffalo%20Ny%20Morning%20Express/Buffalo%20NY%20Morning%20Express%201910%20pdf/Newspapers%20Buffalo%20NY%20Morning%20Express%201910%20-%202709.PDF |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> and her various activities were noted thankfully.<ref>{{cite news |title=Johnstown, NW |author=Ella C. Quant. |volume=1 |issue=5 |newspaper=Star of the West |page=14 |date=June 5, 1910 |url=http://starofthewest.info/viewer.erb?vol=01&page=98 |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref>
A July issue of ''Post Magazine'' included the article "On the Equivocation of 'Matter'",<ref>{{cite news |title=Sunday's post magazine |newspaper=Pittsburgh Daily Post |location=Pittsburgh, PA|page=1 |date=July 13, 1910 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2163824/bahai_mary_hanford_ford_article/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> which was also published in the ''Buffalo Courier''.<ref>{{cite news |title=An Equivocation on "Matter" |author=Mary Hanford Ford |newspaper=Buffalo Courier |location=Buffalo, NY|page=13 |date=July 17, 1910 |url=http://fultonhistory.com/Newspapers%2021/Buffalo%20NY%20Courier/Buffalo%20Ny%20Courier%201910/Buffalo%20Ny%20Courier%201910%20-%204317.pdf |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref>
Starting in 1910, the head of the Baháʼí Faith, ʻAbdu'l-Bahá, was on various stages of a [[ʻAbdu'l-Bahá's journeys to the West|journey]] once freed from arrest. During the period in Paris there is record of a phone call – the caller may have been Ford.<ref>{{cite journal |author=Wendi Momen |title=ʻAbdu'l-Bahá's Encounter with Modernity During His Western Travels|journal=Lights of 'Irfán |volume=13 |editor=Iraj Ayman |pages=273–307 |isbn=978-3-942426-12-1 |date=2012 |url=http://irfancolloquia.org/pdf/lights13_momen_modernity.pdf |access-date=May 10, 2015}}</ref> Ford was in Paris and was happy to see him free.<ref name=InParis>{{cite news |author=Mary Hanford Ford |title=With Abdul-Baha in Paris |newspaper=Star of the West |volume=2 |issue=14 |pages=3–10 |date=November 23, 1911 |url=http://starofthewest.info/viewer.erb?vol=02&page=227 |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=It is possible to so adjust..., Words of Abdul-Baha from notes of Mrs. Mary Hanford Ford, taken in Paris, France, 1911 |newspaper=Star of the West |volume=7 |issue=18 |pages=188 |date=February 7, 1917 |url=http://starofthewest.info/viewer.erb?vol=07&page=188 |access-date=May 19, 2015}}</ref> She left a description and recorded some of the talks and described the atmosphere of how being around him was for people. She spent two weeks there with ʻAbdu'l-Bahá.<ref name=Whitehead/>
A month before ʻAbdu'l-Bahá's arrival in America, Ford wrote an article profiling the history of the religion, its presence in parts of the world, and ʻAbdu'l-Bahá himself, which covered over half a page in the ''New York Sun''.<ref>{{cite news |author=Mary Hanford Ford |title=Abdul Baha, head of new religion, coming here |newspaper=The Sun |location=New York, NY|pages=2 |date=March 17, 1912 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2163360/half_page_story_on_abdulbaha_by_mary/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> ʻAbdu'l-Bahá left America for Britain in December, France in January, Germany in April. In February 1913 Ford's book ''Oriental Rose'' was being read and commented on by [[Marshall Black]] in prison in California.<ref>{{cite news |title=Philosophy aids Marshall Black in jail |newspaper=Oakland Tribune |location=Oakland, CA|pages=23 |date=February 2, 1913 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2163364/bahais_noted_marshall_black_i_a/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref>
Before ʻAbdu'l-Bahá left America he commented in the progress of women's equality, noting "Demonstrations of force, such as are now taking place in England, are neither becoming nor effective in the cause of womanhood and quality".<ref>{{cite book|author = ʻAbdu'l-Bahá|title = The Promulgation of Universal Peace|url = http://reference.bahai.org/en/t/ab/PUP/pup-95.html|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20120229061414/http://reference.bahai.org/en/t/ab/PUP/pup-95.html|archive-date = February 29, 2012|edition = 2nd|orig-year = 1922|year = 1982|publisher = US Baháʼí Publishing Trust|isbn = 978-0-87743-172-5|page = 283 }}</ref> A few months later, in March–April 1913, a suffrage women's organization, the [[Women's Social and Political Union]] (WSPU) in Britain, was broken up by [[Scotland Yard]].<ref name=Glasgow>{{cite web |author=Ellen Cole |title=Women's Suffrage An introduction to primary resources in Glasgow University Library's Special Collections - 1914: Mary Richardson damages Wallace Collection painting with an axe |publisher=Glasgow University Library's Special Collections |date=December 2007 |url=http://special.lib.gla.ac.uk/teach/suffrage/suffrage.html |access-date=May 10, 2015}}</ref><ref name=Spy>{{cite news |author=Dominic Casciani |title=Spy pictures of suffragettes revealed |newspaper=BBC News |date=October 3, 2003 |url=http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/uk_news/magazine/3153024.stm |access-date=May 10, 2015}}</ref> Led by [[Flora Drummond]] the group tried Ford in absentia in April for the failure of their plans.<ref>{{cite news |title=Militants accuse members as spies |newspaper=New York Times |location=New York, NY|page=? |date=April 26, 1913 |url=https://query.nytimes.com/gst/abstract.html?res=9B06E3DD103BE633A25755C2A9629C946296D6CF |access-date=May 3, 2015}}</ref> Arrests and releases took place in February and March and some of the incidents were coordinated by WPSU itself.<ref>{{cite book |author=Elizabeth Crawford |title=The Women's Suffrage Movement: A Reference Guide 1866-1928 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ygXwlK_mj50C&pg=PT1454 |date=September 2, 2003 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=1-135-43401-8 |pages=1454–1457}}</ref> Ford was present and acting for a release, and coverage did appear in American newspapers,<ref>* {{cite news |title=Ask for Laughlin's head |newspaper=The New York Times |location=New York, NY|page=2 |date=April 4, 1913 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2449650/bahai_mary_hanford_ford_in/ |access-date=May 20, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=Emerson girl's friends score first success |newspaper=Reading Times |location=Reading, PA|page=10 |date=April 4, 1913 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2449632/bahai_mary_hanford_ford_aiding_in/ |access-date=May 20, 2015}}</ref> but she claimed she knew nothing of any of the charges the group had brought against her. Newspapers covered the report in many places,<ref>* {{cite news |title=Court-Martial to try American Suffragette |newspaper=Atlanta Constitution |location=Atlanta, GA |page=11? |date=April 26, 1913 |url=http://www.leofrank.org/library/atlanta-constitution-issues/1913/atlanta-constitution-april-26-1913-14-pages-combined.pdf |access-date=May 3, 2015 }} * {{cite news |title=Women holding court martial |newspaper=The Bisbee Daily Review |location=Bisbee, AZ |page=1 |date=April 26, 1913 |url=http://adnp.azlibrary.gov/cdm/ref/collection/sn84024827/id/35242 |access-date=May 3, 2015 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160303222233/http://adnp.azlibrary.gov/cdm/ref/collection/sn84024827/id/35242 |archive-date=March 3, 2016 |url-status=dead }} * {{cite news |title=The accused conspicuous by absence |newspaper=Altoona Tribune |location=Altoona, PA |page=1 |date=April 26, 1913 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2347063/bahai_mary_hanford_ford_in_british/ |access-date=May 3, 2015 }} * {{cite news |title=Treason in the militant camp |newspaper=The Coffeyville Daily Journal |location=Coffeyville, KS |page=1 |date=April 26, 1913 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2347079/bahai_mary_hanford_ford_mentioned_in/ |access-date=May 3, 2015 }} * {{cite news |title=Suffrage court-martial called |newspaper=Morning Oregonian |location=Portland, OR |page=1 |date=April 26, 1913 |url=http://oregonnews.uoregon.edu/lccn/sn83025138/1913-04-26/ed-1/seq-1.pdf |access-date=May 3, 2015 }} * {{cite news |title=Court martial for American Suffragette |newspaper=The Gazette Times |location=Pittsburgh, PA |page=1 |date=April 26, 1913 |url=https://news.google.com/newspapers?nid=1126&dat=19130426&id=VmwxAAAAIBAJ&pg=2155,5785103&hl=en |access-date=May 4, 2015 }} * {{cite news |title=Traitor in the militant camp |newspaper=The Montreal Gazette |location=Montreal, Ontario |page=1 |date=April 26, 1913 |url=https://news.google.com/newspapers?nid=1946&dat=19130426&id=5RYqAAAAIBAJ&pg=5454,3108365&hl=en |access-date=May 4, 2015 }} * {{cite news |title=Miss Kenny up on ancient law |newspaper=The Saratogian |location=Saratoga Springs, NY |page=3 |date=April 26, 1913 |url=http://fultonhistory.com/Newspapers%2021/Saratoga%20Springs%20NY%20Saratogian/Saratoga%20Springs%20NY%20Saratogian%201913/Saratoga%20Springs%20NY%20Saratogian%201913%20-%200711.pdf |access-date=May 4, 2015 }}</ref> including places Ford had given talks - Independence, Kansas,<ref>{{cite news |title=Oh, Girls! She's a traitor |newspaper=The Evening Star |location=Independence, KS|page=4 |date=April 26, 1913 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2347096/bahai_mary_hanford_ford_mentioned_in/ |access-date=May 3, 2015}}</ref> Fort Wayne, Indiana,<ref>{{cite news |title=London suffragettes out of jail try American sister for treason |newspaper=The Fort Wayne Journal-Gazette |location=Fort Wayne, IN|page=1 |date=April 26, 1913 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2347101/bahai_mary_hanford_ford_mentioned_in/ |access-date=May 3, 2015}}</ref> and Boston, Massachusetts.<ref>{{cite news |title=Militants try an American |newspaper=Boston Evening Transcript |location=Boston, MA|page=4 |date=April 26, 1913 |url=https://news.google.com/newspapers?nid=2249&dat=19130426&id=I64-AAAAIBAJ&pg=5398,5115936&hl=en |access-date=May 4, 2015}}</ref> There had in fact been some tensions among suffrage workers in Britain because some had attempted an arson and bombing campaign and the group had been infiltrated by women working for Scotland Yard<ref>{{cite web |author=Janet Painter |title=Emmeline Pankhurst, 1858-1928; The Suffragette Movement and Her Connection to Epsom |publisher=Epsom and Ewell Local and Family History Centre |date=January 2012 |url=http://www.epsomandewellhistoryexplorer.org.uk/Pankhurst.html |access-date=May 10, 2015}}</ref> who passed on warnings of the violence of the group.<ref name=Glasgow/><ref name=Spy/> Late April coverage vindicated Ford and that her involvement had been through diplomatic channels only.<ref>* {{cite news |title=Suffragette Headquarters (continued ...) |newspaper=Fort Wayne Daily News |location=Fort Wayne, IN|page=12 |date=April 30, 1913 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2449691/bahai_mary_hanford_ford_innocent_of/ |access-date=May 20, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=Raided suffs headquarters |newspaper=The Winfield Daily Free Press |location=Winfield, KS|page=1 |date=April 30, 1913 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2449676/bahai_mary_hanford_ford_innocent_of/ |access-date=May 20, 2014}} * {{cite news |title=Suffrage talks now mean jail |newspaper=The Washington Times |location=Washington, District of Columbia |page=1 |date=April 30, 1913 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2449704/bahai_mary_hanford_ford_innocent_of/ |access-date=May 20, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=Headquarters of militants raided |newspaper=The Evening Missourian |location=Columbia, MO|page=1 |date=April 30, 1913 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2449706/bahai_mary_hanford_ford_innocent_of/ |access-date=May 20, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=Raid opens war on suffragettes |newspaper=The Topeka Daily Capital |location=Topeka, KS|page=12 |date=May 1, 1913 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2347060/bahai_mary_hanford_ford_mentioned_in/ |access-date=May 3, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=Writer declares barbaric cruelty is lot of suffragettes in British prisons |newspaper=Oakland Tribune |location=Oakland, CA|page=10 |date=May 11, 1913 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2449712/bahai_mary_hanford_ford_linked_to/ |access-date=May 3, 2015}}</ref> While this news unfolded in later April Ford attended and addressed the national convention of Baháʼís in New York.<ref>{{cite news |title=Public meetings of the Fifth Annual Convention of Bahai Temple Unity, New York City, April 26-29, 1913 |author=Joseph H. Hannen |newspaper=Star of the West |volume=4 |issue=5 |pages=83–86 (see pp. 85–86) |date=June 5, 1913 |url=http://starofthewest.info/viewer.erb?vol=04&page=85 |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> Later in June she was noted speaking on "Abdul Baha's teaching on Immortality", her first known talk on the religion to a general audience, to the "[[Afrocentric education#19th and early 20th century|Negro Society for Historical Research]]" cofounded by [[John Edward Bruce]] and [[Arturo Alfonso Schomburg]].<ref>{{cite news |title=Teaching of Immortality |newspaper=The Yonkers Statesman |location=Yonkers, NY|pages=6 |date=June 23, 1913 |url=http://fultonhistory.com/Newspaper%2018/Yonkers%20NY%20Statesman/Yonkers%20NY%20Statesman%201913/Yonkers%20NY%20Statesman%201913%20-%201806.pdf |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref>
After ʻAbdu'l-Bahá returned to Egypt almost ending his travels outside of Palestine he sent a tablet/letter responding to a letter from Ford on October 23, 1913.<ref name=DLH>{{cite web |author=Duane L. Herrmann |title=ʻAbdu'l-Baha Writes to Kansas City |website=bahai-library.com |date=2002 |url=http://bahai-library.com/herrmann_abdul-baha_kansas_city |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref>
==War years==
===In California=== In 1914 Ford, mentioning she had been to [[Florence, Italy]], addressed an audience in [[Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania]] on the brewing "European War" as it was called.<ref>{{cite news |title=To talk of war ...|newspaper=Pittsburgh Daily Post |location=Pittsburgh, PA|page=5 |date=September 20, 1914 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2173110/bahai_mary_hanford_ford_talk_on/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref>
In January through March 1915 Ford gave a series of talks in Kansas City.<ref>* {{cite news |title =The monthly meeting of the Kansas City Equal Suffrage Association ...|newspaper =Kansas City Star |location =Kansas City, MO |page =6c |date =January 24, 1915 }} * {{cite news |title =The second in the series ...|newspaper =Kansas City Star |location =Kansas City, MO |page =5c |date =January 31, 1915 }} * {{cite news |title =Mrs. Mary Hanford Ford is giving ...|newspaper =Kansas City Star |location =Kansas City, MO |page =4c |date =February 21, 1915 }} * {{cite news |title =The Kansas City Athenaeum ...|newspaper =Kansas City Star |location =Kansas City, MO |page =4c |date =March 7, 1915 }} * {{cite news |title =Mrs. Mary Hanford Ford will give ...|newspaper =Kansas City Star |location =Kansas City, MO |page =4c |date =March 21, 1915 }}</ref> In April the Baháʼís organized a presence at the [[Panama–Pacific International Exposition]] and Ford was a keynote speaker.<ref>* {{cite news |title=Ceremonies at the official reception given to the International Bahai Congress by the Directorate of the Panama-Pacific International Exposition in Festival Hall, Exposition Grounds, Saturday, April 24, 1915 |newspaper=Star of the West |volume=6 |issue=4 |page=(see pp. 30–34; 38) |date=May 17, 1915 |url=http://starofthewest.info/viewer.erb?vol=06&page=30 |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=Corrections |newspaper=Star of the West |volume=6 |issue=6 |page=49 |date=June 24, 1915 |url=http://starofthewest.info/viewer.erb?vol=06&page=49 |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=Truly I say the believers of God in that Congress ... |author=ʻAbdu'l-Bahá |author2=trans Mirza Ahmad Sohrab |newspaper=Star of the West |volume=7 |issue=10 |page=95 |date=September 8, 1916 |url=http://starofthewest.info/viewer.erb?vol=07&page=95 |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> Her talk, as part of a review of the entire proceedings, was included in ''Baháʼí World'', a text bringing together developments of the religion from around the world, in volume 8.<ref name=BWindex>{{cite web |title=The Baha'i World Contents of Volumes I through XX in chronological order Ridvan 1924 through Ridvan 1992 |website=Bahai-Library.com |date=October 27, 2012 |url=http://bahai-library.com/pdf/bw/index_bw_contents_chronological_1-20.pdf |access-date=May 19, 2015}}</ref> This was followed by a Baháʼí meeting during [[Ridván]] at the home of Helen S. Goodall in [[Oakland, California]] Ford attended and reported on in ''[[Star of the West (Baháʼí magazine)|Star of the West]]'', among the earliest national publications of the religion.<ref>{{cite news |title=The Feast of Rizwan, at the home of Mrs. Helen S. Goodall, Oakland, Ca, April 21, 1915 |author=Mary Hanford Ford |newspaper=Star of the West |volume=6 |issue=5 |pages=35–37 |date=June 5, 1915 |url=http://starofthewest.info/viewer.erb?vol=06&page=35 |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> In September Ford spoke in San Mateo.<ref>{{cite news |title =Miss Mary Hanford Ford will address ...|newspaper =San Jose Mercury News |location =San Jose, CA |page =16B |date =September 17, 1915 }}</ref> Ford continued in Oakland in October with a month of Monday lectures on art hosted by a group of women including Florence Breed Khan, or as she was called, Mrs. Ali Kuli Khan.<ref>{{cite news |title=Lectures to stimulate art love |newspaper=Oakland Tribune |location=Oakland, CA|page=11 |date=October 6, 1915 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2160136/lecture_series_with_bahai_mme_ali_kuli/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> The Khan's had hit a "rough patch" in their marriage exacerbated by subterfuge by Persian relations during which Ford became concerned as she had introduced them.<ref name=Arches/> While there she was visited by her son Roland.<ref>{{cite news |title=Mr. and Mrs. Roland Ford have returned ...|newspaper=Kansas City Times |location=Kansas City, MO|page=5 |date=October 19, 1915 |url=https://newspaperarchive.com/us/kansas/kansas-city/kansas-city-times/1915/10-19/page-5 |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> Ford visited Portland and Seattle Baháʼís,<ref name=Whitehead/> and then another series of talks was noted in November back in Oakland,<ref>{{cite news |title=Exposition art is theme of lectures |newspaper=Oakland Tribune |location=Oakland, CA|page=11 |date=November 1, 1915 |url=https://newspaperarchive.com/us/california/oakland/oakland-tribune/1915/11-01/page-11 |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> as well as a separate talk on peace,<ref>{{cite news |title=Society hears a message of peace |newspaper=Oakland Tribune |location=Oakland, CA|page=6 |date=November 13, 1915 |url=https://newspaperarchive.com/us/california/oakland/oakland-tribune/1915/11-13/page-6 |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> and still another on art at the [[Palace of Fine Arts]].<ref>* {{cite news |title =Intimate Address on Fine Arts Exhibits. Mrs. Mary Hanford Ford Gives Illuminating Talk to Los Gatos History Club |newspaper =San Jose Mercury News |location =San Jose, CA |page =9 |date =November 25, 1915 }} * {{cite news |title=Intimate Address on Fine Arts Exhibits. Mrs. Mary Hanford Ford Gives Illuminating Talk to Los Gatos History Club |newspaper=San Jose Mercury News |location =San Jose, CA|page=9 |date = November 25, 1915 }}</ref> Come January 1916 another series was launched in Oakland and highlighted the new architectural forms in the West of the US.<ref>{{cite news |title=New architecture created by West(at beginning and end of article) |newspaper=Oakland Tribune |location=Oakland, CA|page=3 |date=January 20, 1916 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2173123/bahai_mary_hanford_ford_talk_series/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> In February Ford lectures for [[Mills College]],<ref>{{cite news |title=Lecture on art |newspaper=Oakland Tribune |location=Oakland, CA|page=4 |date=February 14, 1916 |url=https://newspaperarchive.com/us/california/oakland/oakland-tribune/1916/02-14/page-4 |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> in March she began a series of talks,<ref name=OT-5-11>{{cite news |title=Attend lecture |newspaper=Oakland Tribune |location=Oakland, CA|page=6 |date=May 11, 1916 |url=https://newspaperarchive.com/us/california/oakland/oakland-tribune/1916/05-11/page-6 |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> and lectured for the Ebell Society club<ref>* {{cite news |title=Mrs. Ford's lecture |newspaper=Oakland Tribune |location=Oakland, CA|page=42 |date=March 5, 1916 |url=https://newspaperarchive.com/us/california/oakland/oakland-tribune/1916/03-05/page-42 |access-date=April 18, 2015}} * {{cite news |title=Mrs Ford's lecture |newspaper=Oakland Tribune |location=Oakland, CA|page=14 |date=March 19, 1916 |url=https://newspaperarchive.com/us/california/oakland/oakland-tribune/1916/03-19/page-14 |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> that extends into May.<ref name=OT-5-11/> Meanwhile, Ford's mother died in March,<ref name=MrsFinney>{{cite news |title=The death of Mrs Finney |newspaper=The Gazette Globe |location=Kansas City, KS|pages=1 |date=March 27, 1916 |url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/2166429/ford_mh__1916_mar_27__kc/ |access-date=April 18, 2015}}</ref> and in April Ford and Mrs. Khan are noted as patronesses of a child theatre.<ref>{{cite news |title=Child actors will present charming play |newspaper=Berkeley Daily Gazette |location=Oakland, CA|page=7 |date=April 29, 1916
